food_subgroup: Low-carbohydrate beef bowl combo with fermented vegetables, tofu, and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for kimchi low-carb gyudon specifically. FOODON_00002765 (beef bowl food product) is the closest parent category covering the gyudon base.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW (Beef dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for gyudon or kimchi gyudon in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to kimchi low-carb gyudon combo. SNOMED CT does not have sufficient granularity for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One combo set comprising approximately 150 g simmered beef with kimchi, 100 g silken tofu, 80 g mixed salad, one half-boiled egg (approximately 50 g), and 100 ml miso soup, totalling approximately 480 g.

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) and component estimation: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled (NDB 23620); Tofu, raw, firm (NDB 16126); Kimchi (NDB 11999); Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (NDB 01129); Lettuce, green leaf, raw (NDB 11253); Miso (NDB 02048). Composite dish estimated by weighted ingredient proportions per 100 g edible portion.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi low-carb gyudon. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy values for each major component (thinly sliced simmered beef, kimchi, firm tofu, mixed salad greens, half-boiled egg, miso soup) weighted by approximate ingredient proportions in a 480 g serving. Beef component uses USDA data for cooked lean beef adjusted for gyudon-style simmering with soy sauce and mirin. Sodium is elevated due to kimchi, miso, and soy-based sauce. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of tofu and low-carbohydrate mixed meals — analogous estimate based on: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Tofu GI ~15; beef GI ~0 (negligible carbohydrate); kimchi GI ~15; mixed salad GI ~15; egg GI ~0; miso soup GI ~40. Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 32 for the low-carbohydrate combination.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi low-carb gyudon. GI is estimated as a weighted composite of component GI values sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The low-carbohydrate nature of this dish (rice replaced by tofu and salad) substantially reduces the overall GI compared to standard gyudon. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 21.6) / 100 ≈ 6.9, rounded to 6.5 accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from kimchi, miso soup, and soy-based simmering sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this dish as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,016 mg per serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced miso soup or sauce may help lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low net carbohydrate content due to rice substitution with tofu and salad
type2_diabetes.guidance: This low-carbohydrate gyudon variant is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as the replacement of rice with tofu and salad substantially reduces the glycemic load to approximately 6.5 per serving, supporting postprandial blood glucose management; the high protein content from beef and tofu also promotes satiety.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from fermented and seasoned components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the moderate saturated fat content (approximately 1.6 g per 100 g) from the beef component and the high sodium load from kimchi and miso; choosing a smaller portion and requesting reduced sauce can help align this meal with heart-healthy dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Low-Carb Gyudon Combo is a Japanese-style beef bowl dish adapted for low-carbohydrate eating, served at Sukiya and similar Japanese fast-casual restaurants in Singapore. Instead of the traditional steamed white rice base, the simmered seasoned beef and kimchi topping is served over a bed of mixed salad greens and silken tofu, dramatically reducing the carbohydrate content of the meal. The beef is thinly sliced and simmered in a dashi, soy sauce, and mirin-based sauce with onions and fermented kimchi, imparting a savoury, slightly tangy, and umami-rich flavour profile. The dish is completed with a half-boiled egg, which adds richness and additional protein, and is served with miso soup or a drink. The texture contrast between the tender simmered beef, soft tofu, crunchy salad, and runny egg yolk makes this a satisfying and nutritionally balanced meal. Clinically, this dish is notable for its low glycemic load (approximately 6.5 per serving) and high protein content (approximately 44 g per serving), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, the sodium content is elevated due to kimchi, miso soup, and the soy-based simmering sauce, which warrants caution for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish provides meaningful amounts of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and calcium from the beef, egg, and tofu components.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for kimchi low-carb gyudon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy component data weighted by approximate ingredient proportions. GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for thinly sliced simmered beef, kimchi, firm tofu, mixed salad greens, half-boiled egg, and miso soup, weighted by approximate proportions in a 480 g serving. No direct approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. Sodium is elevated (approximately 420 mg per 100 g; approximately 2,016 mg per serving) due to kimchi, miso soup, and soy-based simmering sauce; this is a key clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. 3. GI is an analogous composite estimate (approximately 32) based on component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI Values, Diabetes Care. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not directly measured for this dish; these values carry higher uncertainty. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON include 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers', 'Drinks & Beverages', and 'Fried Chicken', which are not relevant to this dish and appear to be platform-level restaurant tags rather than dish-specific tags; these have been excluded from the output. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore does not hold a MUIS halal certification as of the knowledge cutoff. 7. The dish is served at Sukiya (Funan) and Funan Mix & Match in Singapore at SGD 10.40.
